label: delphi-r1-p2
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the leading cases and how courts have actually applied the rule to messy facts.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not, a court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge violates article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements -- most plausibly by striking the \"conservation-incentive\" increment above the documented 1.6x cost differential and/or the portion of revenue funneled into rebates that the district itself concedes mostly benefit non-payers -- while a full, top-to-bottom invalidation of the entire $4.1M charge is somewhat less likely given the genuine cost-of-service anchor the district does have.","rationales":["The rate study's own admission that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly from cost allocation tracks Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano's core holding that tiered rates must reflect actual marginal cost differences, not behavior-shaping price signals, and section 6(b) places the burden of proving compliance on the agency, not the challenger.","The district's concession that the 55%-of-revenue rebate program 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' is a serious, largely self-inflicted vulnerability under article XIII D section 6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality cap and section 6(b)(2)'s restriction on fee-revenue use, since money extracted from high-tier payors funds other ratepayers' private turf/appliance improvements rather than costs attributable to the payors' own service.","The documented 1.6x marginal-cost finding (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) gives the district a genuine, non-pretextual cost-of-service anchor that post-Capistrano authority crediting real cost studies (verify current treatment of Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency and any more recent tiered-rate decisions) suggests can sustain at least the cost-justified portion of the surcharge, making full invalidation less likely than partial invalidation.","The 30% debt-service allocation for the recycled-water plant is plausibly defensible as tied to the same peaking/drought-reserve cost drivers the rate study identifies for high-tier usage, so a court may sever and uphold that slice even while striking the incentive-pricing premium and the cross-subsidized rebate slice.","The conservation mandate under article X, section 2 and Water Code section 375 is unlikely to override Prop 218's specific proportionality text, since Capistrano itself treated the general conservation policy as insufficient to excuse a rate structure from section 6(b)'s cost-based limits, undercutting the district's primary structural defense."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(3)","Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586","Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105","Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 271 (verify holding and current status)","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26 exemptions, verify applicability to rebuttal that surcharge is a tax)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the actual numeric relationship between the $0.85 surcharge and the 1.6x cost differential is close enough that a court would treat the whole rate as substantially cost-based (with 'conservation-incentive pricing' as mere rhetorical framing), versus treating the district's own characterization as a dispositive admission that a material portion of the rate is untethered to cost -- the facts as given don't specify the base-tier rate needed to test the 1.6x math, so this turns on record evidence not fully presented in the hypothetical."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not, a court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge violates article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements -- most plausibly by striking the "conservation-incentive" increment above the documented 1.6x cost differential and/or the portion of revenue funneled into rebates that the district itself concedes mostly benefit non-payers -- while a full, top-to-bottom invalidation of the entire $4.1M charge is somewhat less likely given the genuine cost-of-service anchor the district does have.
rationales: The rate study's own admission that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly from cost allocation tracks Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano's core holding that tiered rates must reflect actual marginal cost differences, not behavior-shaping price signals, and section 6(b) places the burden of proving compliance on the agency, not the challenger.

The district's concession that the 55%-of-revenue rebate program 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' is a serious, largely self-inflicted vulnerability under article XIII D section 6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality cap and section 6(b)(2)'s restriction on fee-revenue use, since money extracted from high-tier payors funds other ratepayers' private turf/appliance improvements rather than costs attributable to the payors' own service.

The documented 1.6x marginal-cost finding (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) gives the district a genuine, non-pretextual cost-of-service anchor that post-Capistrano authority crediting real cost studies (verify current treatment of Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency and any more recent tiered-rate decisions) suggests can sustain at least the cost-justified portion of the surcharge, making full invalidation less likely than partial invalidation.

The 30% debt-service allocation for the recycled-water plant is plausibly defensible as tied to the same peaking/drought-reserve cost drivers the rate study identifies for high-tier usage, so a court may sever and uphold that slice even while striking the incentive-pricing premium and the cross-subsidized rebate slice.

The conservation mandate under article X, section 2 and Water Code section 375 is unlikely to override Prop 218's specific proportionality text, since Capistrano itself treated the general conservation policy as insufficient to excuse a rate structure from section 6(b)'s cost-based limits, undercutting the district's primary structural defense.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586
Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105
Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 271 (verify holding and current status)
Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375
Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26 exemptions, verify applicability to rebuttal that surcharge is a tax)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the actual numeric relationship between the $0.85 surcharge and the 1.6x cost differential is close enough that a court would treat the whole rate as substantially cost-based (with 'conservation-incentive pricing' as mere rhetorical framing), versus treating the district's own characterization as a dispositive admission that a material portion of the rate is untethered to cost -- the facts as given don't specify the base-tier rate needed to test the 1.6x math, so this turns on record evidence not fully presented in the hypothetical.
